upgradeInfo.migrationMarkdown:
Introducing GPT-5.4

Codex just got an upgrade with GPT-5.4, our most capable model for professional work. It outperforms prior models while being more token efficient, with notable improvements on long-running tasks, tool calling, computer use, and frontend development.

Learn more: https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4

You can always keep using GPT-5.3-Codex if you prefer.

content.text: Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is
Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is

msg.message: Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is

content.text:
Canada is a vast country in North America known for its size, political stability, multicultural society, abundant natural resources, and distinctive mix of British, French, and Indigenous influences. It is the second-largest country in the world by total area, stretching from the Atlantic Ocean in the east to the Pacific Ocean in the west and northward into the Arctic. It shares the longest international land border in the world with the United States.

Canada is a federal parliamentary democracy and a constitutional monarchy. That means it has both a prime minister, who leads the government, and a monarch, represented domestically by the governor general. Its political system combines British parliamentary traditions with a federal structure that divides powers between the national government and the provinces. The country is made up of ten provinces and three territories. The provinces generally have stronger constitutional powers than the territories, especially over areas like education and health care.

The capital of Canada is Ottawa, located in Ontario near the border with Quebec. The largest city is Toronto, which is the country’s main financial center and one of the most multicultural cities in the world. Other major cities include Montreal, Vancouver, Calgary, Edmonton, Quebec City, Winnipeg, and Halifax. Each has its own character: Montreal is known for its French-speaking culture and arts scene, Vancouver for its Pacific setting and mild climate, Calgary for energy and proximity to the Rockies, and Quebec City for its historic architecture and strong Francophone identity.

Canada has two official languages at the federal level: English and French. English is the majority language in most of the country, while French is the dominant language in Quebec and is also widely spoken in parts of New Brunswick and Ontario. Official bilingualism is an important part of Canadian identity and federal administration. Beyond English and French, Canada is home to many immigrant languages and Indigenous languages, reflecting both its long pre-colonial history and its modern immigration patterns.

A defining feature of Canada is its Indigenous heritage. Long before European settlement, the land was home to diverse Indigenous peoples, including First Nations, Inuit, and Metis communities, each with distinct cultures, governance systems, languages, and traditions. Indigenous peoples remain central to Canada’s identity, politics, and cultural life. At the same time, Canada’s history includes colonization, displacement, broken treaties, and the residential school system, which caused profound harm. In recent decades, truth, reconciliation, land rights, and Indigenous self-determination have become major national issues.

Historically, European exploration and colonization began mainly through French and British expansion. France established settlements along the St. Lawrence River and in parts of Atlantic Canada, while Britain later gained control over much of New France after the Seven Years’ War. These competing colonial legacies still shape the country. The tension and accommodation between English-speaking and French-speaking Canada remain one of the core themes of Canadian history. Confederation took place in 1867, when several colonies joined to form the Dominion of Canada, which then expanded westward and northward over time.

Geographically, Canada is extraordinarily diverse. It contains mountains, prairies, forests, shield rock, tundra, Arctic archipelagos, and immense freshwater systems. The Rocky Mountains dominate much of the west, while the Prairie provinces are known for flat agricultural land and energy production. Central Canada includes the populous corridor around the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River. The Canadian Shield covers a huge part of the country and is rich in minerals, lakes, and boreal forest. The far north is marked by Arctic climate, sparse population, and strategic significance.

Canada is famous for its natural beauty. Banff and Jasper National Parks, Niagara Falls, the Cabot Trail, the Bay of Fundy, the northern lights, and countless lakes and forests are major attractions. The country has a deep global association with wilderness, though in reality most Canadians live in urban areas relatively close to the southern border. Nature still plays an outsized role in national identity, recreation, tourism, and environmental policy.

The climate varies dramatically. Southern parts of British Columbia can be relatively mild, while the Prairies experience harsh winters and hot summers. Central Canada has four pronounced seasons, and northern Canada can be extremely cold for much of the year. Snow, ice, and winter sports are strongly associated with Canadian life, though regional differences are large enough that climate experience can vary significantly from one province to another.

Canada’s economy is advanced, diversified, and deeply tied to global trade, especially with the United States. Major sectors include natural resources, manufacturing, technology, finance, agriculture, transportation, and services. Canada is a significant producer of oil, natural gas, potash, timber, uranium, nickel, gold, and other resources. It is also a major exporter of wheat and other agricultural goods. While resource wealth has long underpinned economic development, services and knowledge industries are also very important in major urban centers.

One of Canada’s best-known public institutions is its health care system. Canadians often take pride in publicly funded health care, though the actual system is administered mainly by provinces and territories, so access and performance can vary regionally. Health care is central to national political identity, often seen as one of the clearest differences between Canada and the United States. At the same time, Canadians debate wait times, staffing shortages, rural access, and system reform.

Canada is widely regarded as an immigrant society. Immigration has played a central role in shaping the country’s population and labor force. The state has long promoted immigration as part of economic growth and demographic strategy, and multiculturalism is an official policy. Rather than expecting total assimilation into a single dominant culture, Canada has tended to present itself as a multicultural mosaic. This idea is sometimes contrasted with the “melting pot” metaphor more often associated with the United States. In practice, of course, integration, discrimination, housing access, and identity politics remain complex and contested.

Canadian culture combines regionalism with national symbols. Common symbols include the maple leaf, the beaver, hockey, the canoe, and the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. Ice hockey has a special place in the national imagination, but Canada is also strong in sports like lacrosse, soccer, basketball, curling, and winter athletics. Canadian musicians, writers, filmmakers, and actors have had global influence. The country has produced internationally known figures in literature, music, comedy, cinema, and science, even if Canadian cultural industries often operate in the shadow of the much larger U.S. market.

Food culture in Canada is varied and regional. Poutine from Quebec is the most internationally famous dish, but there are many others: butter tarts, Nanaimo bars, tourtiere, bannock, Montreal-style bagels, Atlantic seafood traditions, prairie grain-based foods, and maple products. Immigration has also transformed Canadian cuisine, especially in cities like Toronto, Vancouver, and Montreal, where food scenes reflect South Asian, Chinese, Caribbean, Middle Eastern, African, and many other influences.

Education levels in Canada are generally high, and the country is known for strong public education and respected universities. Institutions such as the University of Toronto, McGill, the University of British Columbia, and others attract students from around the world. Research is important in areas such as medicine, artificial intelligence, natural resources, environmental science, and engineering.

In international affairs, Canada has often cultivated an image of moderation, diplomacy, peacekeeping, and multilateral cooperation. It is a member of NATO, the G7, the Commonwealth, La Francophonie, and other major international organizations. Canada’s global image has historically emphasized civility and compromise, though like all countries it also faces criticism and contradictions in foreign policy, defense, trade, and climate commitments.

Environmental issues are especially significant in Canada because of its large landmass, Arctic territory, forests, freshwater reserves, and resource industries. Climate change affects the country in many ways, including Arctic warming, wildfires, flooding, melting permafrost, and pressures on ecosystems and infrastructure. Canada is therefore often caught between its environmental ambitions and its role as a major energy producer.

Canadian identity is sometimes described less as a single unified culture and more as a negotiated balance: English and French, Indigenous and settler, urban and rural, Atlantic and Pacific, continental and Arctic, local and global. That balance is not always smooth. Debates over Quebec nationalism, Western alienation, Indigenous sovereignty, immigration, affordability, and federal-provincial power have all shaped the country. Yet this constant negotiation is also part of what makes Canada politically and culturally distinctive.

In everyday global perception, Canada is often associated with politeness, safety, order, and scenic landscapes. These stereotypes contain some truth but oversimplify a much more complicated country. Canada is wealthy but faces inequality. It is multicultural but still deals with racism and exclusion. It celebrates reconciliation while still confronting the legacy of colonialism. It is resource-rich but environmentally vulnerable. It is geographically enormous yet heavily concentrated along a narrow southern band.

Taken as a whole, Canada is best understood as a large, complex, and regionally varied federation whose identity has been shaped by Indigenous presence, colonial history, bilingualism, immigration, and geography. It is both highly modern and deeply influenced by historical compromise. Its reputation for stability comes not from simplicity, but from its long habit of managing difference through institutions, negotiation, and gradual change.

Nature still plays an outsized role in national identity, recreation, tourism, and environmental policy.

The climate varies dramatically. Southern parts of British Columbia can be relatively mild, while the Prairies experience harsh winters and hot summers. Central Canada has four pronounced seasons, and northern Canada can be extremely cold for much of the year. Snow, ice, and winter sports are strongly associated with Canadian life, though regional differences are large enough that climate experience can vary significantly from one province to another.

Canada’s economy is advanced, diversified, and deeply tied to global trade, especially with the United States. Major sectors include natural resources, manufacturing, technology, finance, agriculture, transportation, and services. Canada is a significant producer of oil, natural gas, potash, timber, uranium, nickel, gold, and other resources. It is also a major exporter of wheat and other agricultural goods. While resource wealth has long underpinned economic development, services and knowledge industries are also very important in major urban centers.

One of Canada’s best-known public institutions is its health care system. Canadians often take pride in publicly funded health care, though the actual system is administered mainly by provinces and territories, so access and performance can vary regionally. Health care is central to national political identity, often seen as one of the clearest differences between Canada and the United States. At the same time, Canadians debate wait times, staffing shortages, rural access, and system reform.

Canada is widely regarded as an immigrant society. Immigration has played a central role in shaping the country’s population and labor force. The state has long promoted immigration as part of economic growth and demographic strategy, and multiculturalism is an official policy. Rather than expecting total assimilation into a single dominant culture, Canada has tended to present itself as a multicultural mosaic. This idea is sometimes contrasted with the “melting pot” metaphor more often associated with the United States. In practice, of course, integration, discrimination, housing access, and identity politics remain complex and contested.

Canadian culture combines regionalism with national symbols. Common symbols include the maple leaf, the beaver, hockey, the canoe, and the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. Ice hockey has a special place in the national imagination, but Canada is also strong in sports like lacrosse, soccer, basketball, curling, and winter athletics. Canadian musicians, writers, filmmakers, and actors have had global influence. The country has produced internationally known figures in literature, music, comedy, cinema, and science, even if Canadian cultural industries often operate in the shadow of the much larger U.S. market.

Food culture in Canada is varied and regional. Poutine from Quebec is the most internationally famous dish, but there are many others: butter tarts, Nanaimo bars, tourtiere, bannock, Montreal-style bagels, Atlantic seafood traditions, prairie grain-based foods, and maple products. Immigration has also transformed Canadian cuisine, especially in cities like Toronto, Vancouver, and Montreal, where food scenes reflect South Asian, Chinese, Caribbean, Middle Eastern, African, and many other influences.

Education levels in Canada are generally high, and the country is known for strong public education and respected universities. Institutions such as the University of Toronto, McGill, the University of British Columbia, and others attract students from around the world. Research is important in areas such as medicine, artificial intelligence, natural resources, environmental science, and engineering.

In international affairs, Canada has often cultivated an image of moderation, diplomacy, peacekeeping, and multilateral cooperation. It is a member of NATO, the G7, the Commonwealth, La Francophonie, and other major international organizations. Canada’s global image has historically emphasized civility and compromise, though like all countries it also faces criticism and contradictions in foreign policy, defense, trade, and climate commitments.

Environmental issues are especially significant in Canada because of its large landmass, Arctic territory, forests, freshwater reserves, and resource industries. Climate change affects the country in many ways, including Arctic warming, wildfires, flooding, melting permafrost, and pressures on ecosystems and infrastructure. Canada is therefore often caught between its environmental ambitions and its role as a major energy producer.

Canadian identity is sometimes described less as a single unified culture and more as a negotiated balance: English and French, Indigenous and settler, urban and rural, Atlantic and Pacific, continental and Arctic, local and global. That balance is not always smooth. Debates over Quebec nationalism, Western alienation, Indigenous sovereignty, immigration, affordability, and federal-provincial power have all shaped the country. Yet this constant negotiation is also part of what makes Canada politically and culturally distinctive.

In everyday global perception, Canada is often associated with politeness, safety, order, and scenic landscapes. These stereotypes contain some truth but oversimplify a much more complicated country. Canada is wealthy but faces inequality. It is multicultural but still deals with racism and exclusion. It celebrates reconciliation while still confronting the legacy of colonialism. It is resource-rich but environmentally vulnerable. It is geographically enormous yet heavily concentrated along a narrow southern band.

Taken as a whole, Canada is best understood as a large, complex, and regionally varied federation whose identity has been shaped by Indigenous presence, colonial history, bilingualism, immigration, and geography. It is both highly modern and deeply influenced by historical compromise. Its reputation for stability comes not from simplicity, but from its long habit of managing difference through institutions, negotiation, and gradual change.
